title: Text Classification Metrics
concept: Classifiers are evaluated using F1-score and confusion matrices to ensure the model isn't just predicting the majority class.

exercises.prompt: What do we want to measure when evaluating a text classifier models like text classifiers are designed to sort sentences. What do 'metrics' help us evaluate about them are we trying to achieve what goal?

options.text: How fast they can type on a keyboard
How many fonts they have installed

exercises.explain: Text classifiers sort text into categories. To know if they are doing a good job, we need ways to measure their performance.

cards.title: Is 95% Accuracy a Lie?
cards.body: If a model is 95% accurate at spotting spam, that sounds amazing! But what if 95% of your emails are regular mail, and the model just guesses 'not spam' for everything? It actually missed every single piece of spam! Let's learn how to spot this trick.

cards.title: Better Metrics
cards.body: Instead of just using basic accuracy, we use the F1-score (which balances finding all correct answers with not making wild guesses) and a Confusion Matrix (a grid showing exactly what the model got right and what it mixed up).

cards.title: The Goalie Test
cards.body: Imagine you are a goalie in soccer. If the other team never shoots, you could just stand still (predict 'no shot') and have 100% accuracy. But you aren't actually a good goalie! To test your true skill, we need to see how you handle real shots (precision) and if you miss any when they do happen (recall). F1-score combines both into one score.

cards.title: The 99% Useless Model
cards.body: If an email filter is tested on 99 safe emails and 1 spam email, a lazy model that labels EVERYTHING as 'safe' is 99% accurate! But its F1-score for detecting spam would be 0, exposing that the model is actually useless at finding spam.

cards.title: Myth: High Accuracy = Great Model
cards.body: A high accuracy score doesn't mean your model is smart. If your dataset is unbalanced (like having way more happy texts than angry ones), a model can get high accuracy by just guessing the most common class every single time.

cards.title: Accuracy vs. F1-Score
cards.body: See how looking at different metrics changes our understanding of the same lazy model's performance on unbalanced data.

right.text: Says '0%' because the model failed to find a single minority class example. Reveals the truth!

left.text: Says '90%' because the model guessed the majority class correctly. Looks great on paper!

exercises.prompt: Which two key metrics does the F1-score balance to give a fair evaluation?

options.text: The number of words and letters

exercises.explain: The F1-score balances precision (how clean the positive predictions are) and recall (how many of the actual positives were caught). It is perfect for unbalanced datasets.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If your text dataset is perfectly balanced (50% positive and 50% negative), accuracy is generally a reliable metric to use.
exercises.explain: If the dataset has an equal number of positive and negative examples, simple accuracy is usually a fine metric to use.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: To see exactly which text categories your model is mixing up, you should look at a ____________.
exercises.explain: A confusion matrix is a table layout that visualizes the performance of a classifier, showing correct and incorrect predictions side-by-side.

exercises.prompt: You test two spam filters on a list of 100 emails (90 Ham, 10 Spam). System A gets 88% accuracy but catches 8 spam emails. System B gets 90% accuracy by labeling every single email as 'Ham'. Which system is behaving like a 'lazy majority-class guesser'?
exercises.explain: System B is using the lazy trick of always guessing 'Ham' because it's the majority class. It is completely blind to actual spam, whereas System A is trying to distinguish between them.

exercises.prompt: Why is a confusion matrix so valuable for evaluating a text classifier compared to just looking at a single accuracy score?

options.text: It only measures how fast the model makes predictions.
It automatically fixes the model's coding bugs.
It breaks down correct and incorrect predictions class by class.
It guarantees that the model will achieve 100% accuracy.

exercises.explain: A confusion matrix is the ultimate tool because it shows you exactly where the model got confused (e.g., calling spam 'safe' or vice versa), rather than hiding these errors behind a single misleading accuracy percentage.
